medications.indication_rationale: Short-acting sublingual GTN relieves anginal episodes by coronary/venous dilatation and can be used prophylactically before known exertion (e.g. before walking up the hill); it is central to stable-angina symptom control and doubles as a safety-netting tool. The source prescribed GTN spray.
medications.dose_route_frequency: GTN spray sublingually at symptom onset or ~5 minutes before the known exertional trigger; if pain persists, repeat, and if not relieved after up to 3 doses over 15 minutes call 000 (as instructed by the prescriber)
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid if using PDE5 inhibitors (e.g. sildenafil); may cause hypotension/headache - sit down when using

medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose antiplatelet therapy reduces cardiovascular events in established coronary disease/stable angina and was initiated in the source. The source gave aspirin 100 mg daily.
medications.dose_route_frequency: aspirin 100 mg orally daily (as per source) - unless allergic or actively bleeding

medications.indication_rationale: Aggressive LDL lowering is essential in coronary disease; the patient is already on a daily statin with good adherence, which should be continued and optimised via the GP. The source noted a daily statin.
medications.dose_route_frequency: continue existing daily statin; optimise intensity as directed by the prescriber (dose not fabricated here)

medications.indication_rationale: Continued blood-pressure control reduces myocardial oxygen demand and cardiovascular risk; the patient is already on a daily antihypertensive with good adherence. The source noted a daily antihypertensive.
medications.dose_route_frequency: continue existing daily antihypertensive (dose set by the prescriber - not fabricated here)

medications.drug_name: immediate/empirical thrombolysis or emergency antiplatelet loading
medications.indication_rationale: Acute ACS/MI treatments are not indicated for a currently pain-free patient with stable, predictable angina and no evidence of an acute event; escalating to emergency ACS therapy would be over-treatment unless the pattern becomes unstable.
contraindications_in_this_case: No evidence of acute coronary syndrome at this presentation

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Dismissing symptoms as indigestion and self-managing without cardiac workup
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Exertional arm-radiating chest pain relieved by rest is angina, not indigestion; failing to arrange cardiac assessment misses established coronary disease.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop and rest before reaching the known pain threshold on the hill, and use the GTN spray prophylactically about 5 minutes before the walk.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Pre-empting the mismatch between myocardial oxygen supply and demand prevents the ischaemic pain cycle, allowing the walk to be completed pain-free and preserving mobility and confidence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not push through the chest pain - stop and rest immediately when it starts, and carry the GTN spray at all times.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Avoiding provocation and treating episodes promptly reduces ischaemic burden and reinforces safe self-management.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Continue taking the daily statin, antihypertensive and the new aspirin consistently.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Consistent antiplatelet, statin and blood-pressure therapy reduces cardiovascular events in coronary disease.

patient_education_points.point: The blood vessels supplying your heart have a stable narrowing. When you walk up the hill, your heart needs more blood, but the narrowed vessel can't deliver it fast enough, causing the heavy squeezing. Resting lowers the demand, and the pain settles. This is called stable angina - it is real heart disease and needs proper cardiac tests, but it is not an active heart attack right now.

Please see your GP within the next 2-3 days for a baseline heart tracing (ECG) and a fast-track referral for a treadmill stress test or CT scan of the heart arteries. Start the daily aspirin (unless you are allergic or bleed easily), carry the GTN spray, and keep taking your cholesterol and blood-pressure tablets.
Use the GTN spray when the pain starts, or 5 minutes before you walk up the hill. Stop walking the moment you feel the heavy sensation - do not push through it.

Call 000 immediately if the chest pain comes on while you are resting or sitting quietly, wakes you from sleep, starts happening more easily than before, or does not go away after 15 minutes and 3 doses of the GTN spray - that can mean the pattern has become unstable or a heart attack is developing.

follow_up_plan.interval: Prompt GP review within 48-72 hours to review a baseline ECG and expedite cardiology diagnostics (exercise stress test or CT coronary angiogram)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Baseline resting ECG, results of exercise tolerance test / CT coronary angiography, symptom frequency and any change in threshold, GTN use and effectiveness, blood pressure and lipid control, and whether anti-anginal therapy or coronary intervention is needed
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Chest pain at rest, pain waking from sleep, pain occurring more frequently or with less exertion, or pain unrelieved by 3 GTN doses over 15 minutes - call 000 immediately.

must_include_items: Recognition of exertional arm-radiating chest pain relieved by rest as stable angina / ischaemic heart disease
Active exclusion of instability (screening for rest pain, nocturnal pain, crescendo pattern) and correct balanced triage to prompt (not emergency) cardiac workup
Prompt GP review with fast-track resting ECG and exercise/CT coronary investigation
Interim GTN (relief and prophylaxis) and aspirin, with continued statin/antihypertensive adherence
Explicit ACS safety-netting (rest pain, night pain, crescendo, or pain unrelieved by 3 GTN doses -> 000)
acceptable_alternatives: 'Angina', 'narrowed heart arteries', 'stable angina / ischaemic heart disease' are acceptable framings
Prompt GP review or a rapid-access cardiology/chest-pain clinic referral are both acceptable provided fast-track ECG/stress testing is secured and ACS safety-netting is given
errors_of_omission: Not recognising the pain as angina / coronary disease
Failing to arrange any cardiac workup (ECG/stress testing)
No GTN or aspirin advised (without contraindication)
No ACS safety-netting for progression to instability
errors_of_commission: Attributing the pain to indigestion or muscle strain and reassuring without workup
Over-triaging a currently stable, pain-free patient to an ambulance/ED as if an acute MI, causing undue alarm and wasting emergency resources
Starting emergency ACS-specific therapy (e.g. thrombolysis) for a stable presentation
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: BALANCED-TRIAGE CASE: A pass requires (1) recognising stable angina / coronary disease and arranging prompt fast-track cardiac workup (ECG plus exercise/CT coronary testing), (2) interim GTN and aspirin with statin/antihypertensive adherence, and (3) explicit ACS safety-netting for progression to instability. Dismissing the pain as indigestion/strain is a dangerous under-triage; escalating this currently stable pattern to emergency transport is over-triage. Correct handling is prompt outpatient workup with robust safety-netting.
